data.title: How To Visualize Your Portfolio By Sector & Asset Class
data.description:
Sign Up For Premium Today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Hello, everyone. I want to do a quick demo today of a helpful feature in portfolios where you can see a visual breakdown of your holdings by either Asset Class or Sector. So to start, in the Health Score tab, you can either scroll to the bottom or you can click the Asset Class button or the Sector button, and this will auto scroll us down here where we can see a visual breakdown of all the holdings in this portfolio.

These graphs are interactive, and you can also open the shelves here to see the percentage weights of particular symbols in each of the relevant categories. So, this is a very helpful way to get a different perspective and look at the composition of the holdings by weight percentage in your portfolio. So, check it out today, and we hope you enjoy.

Sign Up For Premium Today!

data.title: Dollarama: Dollar Store Trading For Benjamin's - Sell
data.description:
Intro

Dollarama Inc. (DOL:CA) (DLMAF) is a Canadian dollar-store operator and practically the counterpart to Dollar General Corporation (DG), despite both not competing directly in most parts of their business activities. While DG is primarily US-focused, DOL has its roots in Canada. However, both did not too long ago enter Mexico, where they for the first time set foot on the same ground.

Unlike DG or Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR), DOL has not experienced a sharp correction between 2023 and 2025, when the US-based firms had to cope with growth challenges, margin pressure, theft, etc.

To the contrary, DOL continues to expand without interruptions. Its stock seems to know only one way, namely up.

The business is strongly growing, don’t get me wrong. I have been watching this name for some years now. But I guess that we have reached a level where the share price has run ahead of fundamentals, trading at an unhealthy premium. I rate DOL a sell due to an elevated risk profile.

Strong execution, but at a hefty price tag

Unlike DG and DLTR, Dollarama is still family-controlled and run. While this is no guarantee for outperformance, I like to see long-term-focused family and/or founders at the steering wheel. Ideally, fully aligned with external shareholders, as is the case here.

Neil Rossy owns more than 5.3 million shares, or the equivalent of around 2% of the company, which has a market cap of CAD 52 billion. So, this is not changing money we are talking about.

Turning to the company, Dollarama has been doing its core business in Canada, more or less similar to what Dollar General is doing in the US, selling stuff at low prices. In the past, for the symbolic dollar or less, the threshold was increased for certain items as an answer to surging inflation. However, the core idea remains the same — selling primarily non-perishable or slowly perishable food, household stuff, decorative items, as well as seasonal stuff at low prices.

What I like about DOL is that the company has significantly higher margins than Dollar General. Gross margins are around 50% higher.

Even more pronounced is the difference on an operating level. DOL has phenomenal margins for a retailer, of rounded up 25%, while DG sees only a fraction of that. For the sake of completeness, DG is slowly recovering from its trough so that it should manage to get back to previous levels in the high single digits, at the minimum. But it will very likely remain far behind DOL.

One core difference between these two is that DOL is strongly expanding internationally. While DG entered Mexico in 2023, Dollarama has been operating and growing in countries like Peru, Guatemala, Colombia, and El Salvador via its equity stake in Dollarcity. In 2024, the 50% share was upped to 60%, stressing the seriousness of this investment. Since 2025, Dollarama also joined Mexico with its first store, for the first time competing directly with DG in a market.

So far, the vast majority of international growth has come from Colombia and Peru. But Mexico will be interesting to watch as it has a population that is around 15% bigger than that of all the other mentioned Latin American countries combined.

But Dollarama is not only expanding in Spanish-speaking countries next to its door. A few days ago, it closed the acquisition of the leading dollar-store operator in Australia (announced in 2025), The Reject Shop, which has been struggling. Interestingly, DOL paid a takeover premium of 110% (!) for a total of $259 million. Management must be very confident to be able to turn this thing around and inject its own growth formula into The Reject Shop.

For the time being, it is not expected that the Australian business will contribute anything meaningful to overall results. Maybe we'll get some guidance next month with the full-year results, but for now, I would treat this latest add-on as an in-progress operation.

For now, DOL has almost 1,700 stores in Canada, one in Mexico, 113 in Guatemala, 80 in El Salvador, 399 in Colombia, and 91 in Peru.

The 401 stores that were acquired in Australia will add much volume, but management will focus on improving operational efficiencies first. If successful, undoubtedly, the torque can be massive, as I believe they paid a good price. However, The Reject Shop has been a very low-margin business so far, as shown in the next chart. Operating margins in the mid- to low-single digits with a falling trend over the last few years.

Time will tell whether they paid a low price and turned this into a money printer or whether the seemingly low price tag was justified for a troubled entity.

The slide below shows what items DOL is selling. Their offerings are spread among 48% consumables, 39% general merch, and 13% seasonal stuff. Most of all the products, namely 61%, are private-label, offering an explanation for why the company has such an efficient cost apparatus with high margins. Around half of the products are sourced domestically, with the rest coming from abroad.

The next slide tells us a bit about the recent business dynamics. Sales grew at an explosive level with 22.2% in the last quarter. Operating and net earnings rose in the double-digits, even though a bit slower due to higher SG&A expenses.

Dollarama is a fast-growing dollar-store operator. Really fast. This justifies a higher valuation to price future growth accordingly. I will come back to this topic further below.

The last point that is important to notice is that growth is to a big part self-financed. Net debt, as shown below, stands at 2.1x to EBITDA, but this figure includes leases for the stores. So effectively, “real” debt measured as net financial debt is significantly lower.

Further proof of Dollarama being a premium company is that it shines with operational excellence compared to other retailers. Their operating margins and ROI are unheard of.

Long-term, the growth story is fully intact. Store count is trending higher, sales follow suit (also on a comparable basis, not just throw more stores), with the bottom line expanding as well.

The company has been opening on average 66 new stores annually over the last ten years. Current plans foresee expanding store count in Canada to 2,200 entities by 2034, from the current almost 1,700, as shown above. So, this growth story seems to be far from finished.

Internationally, we have obviously much room to grow. Canada is likely the most mature market, but DOL still intends to expand meaningfully.

Its Latin American opportunity is vast. With less than half of the total stores compared to Canada but a six times higher population, the white space is clearly there. Even if only half as successful as in Canada, there’s plenty of opportunity. In Australia, the store count is currently only a quarter compared to Canada. Despite the population being a third lower than in Canada, even their growth through higher store count looks possible, on top of turning The Reject Shop from an ultra-low-margin business into a solid contributor.

With that said, what’s the price tag for this promising growth story, you might ask. This is a question I ask myself when looking at strongly growing companies like DOL.

Unfortunately, the market cap is a hefty CAD 52 billion. Net financial debt without leases is around CAD 2 billion for an enterprise value of around CAD 54 billion. Despite having high margins and generating robust free cash flow, we only come out at CAD 1.5 billion pre-leasing payments and a bit less than CAD 1.4 billion after leasing for FCF.

The EV / FCF is around 40x, or the current FCF yield is below 3%.

This is a too-aggressive valuation for me. Even at current growth rates of above 20% (for sales). At this pace, DOL could double in size around every three years. This means, in 2029, the expected FCF yield would still be just 6%. I would be justifiable even if I knew that DOL keeps on compounding for longer than these three years at this pace.

However, this is a bold assumption. What has worked in the past does not have to work in the future. Respectively, the bigger the company becomes, the harder sustaining the growth rates. In my view, at a minimum, a doubling in operations is already priced in. The problem is, what if growth slows down? We have observed that with Dollar General between 2023 and 2025, where sales growth compressed strongly to low single-digit territory, while at the same time, margins came down.

The stock was likewise valued at a perfect north of 30x multiples. Surprisingly, the market did not like the step on the brakes, sending DG down from $250 to well below $100 within the next quarters, before the recovery set in.

My fear from a risk and reward perspective is that DOL could face a similar outcome if something stops running perfectly. DOL today is even higher valued on a multiple basis than DG was at its high. In my view, it very well deserves a premium compared to DG, as DOL has much higher margins and a clearer growth path. That’s not the issue.

But if something breaks, even if only temporarily, DOL stock could crater brutally. Challenges to master are plenty, starting with the turnaround in Australia that could either flop or just take longer than thought. In Canada, market potential could prove to be lower than thought, and/or consumers could remain cautious due to ongoing cost-of-living challenges. Mexico and the other Latin American countries could also lead to slower-than-expected growth for whatever reason.

My problem is that DOL stock is priced perfectly. I do not like that. At 20x multiples, I would be very fine with the setup.

But not at 40x. Sell, waiting for a better entry point.

To prove that DOL is not entirely immune to falling stock prices, we just need to take a look at the year 2018, where temporary growth slowed down. From high to low, DOL shares got cut in half. From lower absolute multiples, but admittedly also on lower business dynamics.

But today, we have more variables, i.e., more risk factors.

Risks to my Thesis

One clear risk factor for my bearish thesis is that Dollarama just continues execution. If it even overdelivers, for example, with its expansion into Mexico / and or Australia, growth could even accelerate, making the currently rich-looking multiple cheaper in hindsight than I am currently assuming.

If consumer sentiment recovers, or if more consumers switch to Dollarama seeking value, this could boost margins and the bottom line for more robust growth.

Dollarama has a clean balance sheet and is aggressively buying back shares. Even though the torque has fallen now strongly (less shares per buyback), these repurchases could still be supportive for the stock price.

Conclusion

I like Dollarama as a business. Execution is strong, and so are the future growth prospects. However, the stock seems richly valued. Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Transfer Earnings Reaction: It's Now Fairly Valued (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It is vitally important that investors keep their outlooks, models, and projections regularly updated. There are a lot of variables at play in capital markets, and they often change quickly. This sentiment is true even for a company as stable and consistent as Energy Transfer, which released its Q4 2025 earnings results on February 17.

This article will serve as a follow-up to Energy Transfer: A Great Long-Term Buying Opportunity. Some key elements to my investment thesis then were:

  1. ET’s increasing focus on moving natural gas leaves investors with a very long investment timeframe, even for a fossil fuels company.

  2. ET is the beneficiary of a massive tailwind in the form of ever-increasing demand for natural gas as a result of the electricity requirements of AI data centers.

  3. ET’s manageable debt load, capital expansion plans, and backlog of projects leave the distribution exceedingly safe.

The price for ET’s common units at publication of my initial coverage was $16.49. Since the initial publication, the unit price has climbed to $18.75 at the time of this writing, meaning investors purchasing then have enjoyed a 13.7% price return and a 15.74% total return, including the February 19 distribution of $.335 per unit. If only all of my investment theses panned out so well.

With the information included in the Q4 earnings call, in my view, the market has effectively re-priced ET’s common units to reflect fair value.

Q4 Earnings Call Updates

One of the aspects of ET that I find most promising is the consistency and predictability of the earnings calls and releases. As the business model is primarily an energy transporter, this means that there is very little variance or surprises come earnings time. This also means that the management team spends most of their earnings calls with a forward-looking approach on projects in development, as opposed to digging into financial results that have already happened. This gives me a greater sense of confidence in the valuation model used to calculate intrinsic value.

Operationally, ET had another excellent quarter of execution by setting volume records across all five of the business segments. ET’s earnings are now exceedingly well-balanced across each of these segments.

Regarding forward guidance for 2026, the management team slightly raised the EBITDA outlook from $17.45 to $17.85 billion, but this was isolated to USA Compression’s acquisition of J-W Power and not a result of unanticipated organic growth. The overall tone of the management team in the earnings call was very bullish, and it seems likely that there could be further increases in guidance as a result of some projects coming online earlier than expected. Co-CEO and Director Thomas Long had this update on the much-anticipated Hugh Brinson project:

Organic growth cap ex came in as expected at $5 to $5.5 billion, with some increased clarity on how these investment dollars will be spent. Two-thirds of this growth cap expenditure is allocated towards increasing natural gas capabilities. Most importantly, the diameter of the pipeline of the Desert Southwest project has been upsized from 42” to 48” as announced in December 2025 and is expected to be online in Q4 2029. Additionally, two new Florida-focused projects were added to an already extensive backlog of natural gas projects that were not included in the Q3 2025 earnings report. The Florida Gas Transmission and the South Florida Project are anticipated to be online in 2028 and 2030, respectively. In the Q3 2025 earnings call, the management team announced a long-term agreement with Oracle, and some gas is already being provided with the pipelines necessary to supply this agreement, which is expected to be completed in mid-2026.

With raised guidance, new capital projects announced, and previously announced projects likely being online ahead of schedule, my takeaways from the Q4 earnings call reflect increased operational clarity, reduced execution risk, and increased confidence in the valuation model. The Q4 earnings call is best summarized through this quote from Mr. Thomas Long:

Updated Valuation

When I initially start covering a company, I like to build in multiple layers of conservatism into my valuations because, as the saying goes, there’s no called strikes in this game. Previously, I had an intrinsic value calculation of $17.12 as a result of an 11% required rate of return and a 3% long-term distribution growth rate. This valuation was purely derived from the expected future distributions, with no factoring in capital appreciation.

While it is both financially and emotionally rewarding to see conservative expectations easily met, setting valuations too low can lead to missed opportunities. The first update to the valuation that needs to be made is the distribution increase of .8% distribution increase announced at the end of January from $.3325 per unit to $.335 per unit. Using the same required rate of return of 11% and the long-term distribution growth rate of 3%, this recently announced distribution hike brings the valuation to $17.25.

The management team has increased the distribution by $.0025 every quarter since Q1 2023 and targets 3%–5% long-term distribution growth. Factoring in the raised 2026 guidance and even more projects added to the backlog, in my view, a 3% long-term distribution growth rate is too conservative. Increasing the distribution growth rate to 3.5%, which is still well below the 5-year distribution growth rate of 7.69%, brings the valuation to $18.49. I consider this to be both a reasonable and conservative valuation, and I will consider this to be the base-case scenario.

However, the other piece of the valuation here is the required rate of return. The previous 11% required rate of return was arrived at by adding an extra risk premium on top of the typical 9% market risk due to the debt load and some vulnerability to energy price fluctuations. The price of WTI has increased by about 10% since starting my initial coverage, and natural gas prices have declined by around 20%. However, this is likely just a return to a more normal price as seasonal demand wanes as winter subsides. The price of natural gas is forecasted to rise through 2027. The Q4 2025 earnings report contained no material updates about debt, outside of reiterating the 4.0 - 4.5x EBITDA leverage target. Increased guidance and the completion of new projects should ease at least some concerns about the debt (see: ‘Updated Risks’ below). With some modest improvements to the risk factors of debt and energy prices, a more bullish take on the sensitivity analysis reduces the required rate of return to 10.5%. With this reduced required rate of return, an increased long-term distribution growth rate of 3.5%, and a new current distribution payout of $.335, I have the updated valuation at $21.33 as the bull case.

Updated Risks

As mentioned, one of the chief concerns for ET unit holders is the sustainability of the debt load. Those concerned about the debt load recently got some good news, as ET’s management team issued some new bonds where the funds raised are designated for existing debt refinancing. The financing costs are 4.55% through 2031, 5.35% through 2036, and 6.3% through 2056. Energy Transfer does have notes worth $1 billion due this year, and the borrowing costs on those notes were 6.05% (issued in 2023). So, for 2026, the management team is reducing the cost of servicing the debt by 1.5% on this $1 billion. Reduced interest expense on the debt should free up some net profit margin to more aggressively pay down debt, finance further expansion projects, or increase the distribution.

Final Thoughts

The Q4 2025 earnings release saw ET’s common units decrease in price by .75%, even though the earnings results were overwhelmingly positive. Despite yesterday’s market reaction to the earnings, Energy Transfer’s common units are undoubtedly more reasonably valued than at the time of my initial article at the end of 2025. With a current market price of $18.61 and an updated base case intrinsic value calculation of $18.49, I believe that the units are fairly valued. A reasonable argument could be made in favor of the bull case scenario, which arrives at a buy price below $21.33. Personally, as I did a lot of cost-averaging in the middle of the $16 range, I will be holding for now and waiting for another obvious buying opportunity below $18.00.

The change in my recommendation from ‘strong buy’ to ‘hold’ is entirely due to the unit price increasing much faster than business performance. The market has simply become more rational regarding ET. If 11% is your required rate of return or the bull case is more convincing, buying ET now is still very much a sound investment. Regardless of which version of the sensitivity analysis you find most compelling, this is a well-managed, highly profitable business with exciting growth prospects that is worth keeping on the watch list.

data.title: Franklin High Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fourth quarter (Q4) of 2025 saw monthly shifts in market sentiment as uncertainties increased in the first part of the quarter. The US federal government went into a shutdown on October 1 as the US Senate was unable to pass a spending bill. This, among other things, delayed or canceled the publication of key economic data leading to a lack of clarity on the economic condition of the United States. The shutdown was lifted in mid-November, and subsequent data showed that economic growth in the United States remained resilient. The US Federal Reserve delivered three consecutive 25-basis-point (bp) cuts in Q4, helping market sentiment to improve in December. Fixed income volatility measures fell toward the end of the quarter, reaching their lowest level since 2021. The US Treasury (UST) yield curve steepened during the quarter as the benchmark 10-year UST yield rose two bps to end the year at 4.17%.
  • Contributors: Security selection in healthcare and packaging.
  • Detractors: Security selection in chemicals and media cable.
  • Outlook: Overall, we remain constructive on high-yield (HY) corporate credit and believe it offers a reasonable loss-adjusted carry but acknowledge that valuations do not offer much upside potential from here.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s security selection contributed to returns led by selection in the healthcare, packaging and finance sectors. In contrast, selection within the chemicals, media cable and wired communication segments of the HY market curbed results.
  • Our industry exposures also helped performance. There were positive contributions from our underweight to technology and retailer sectors as well as an overweight to finance-related issues. Overweight exposure to building and underweight food and beverage names and overweight to the chemical sector curbed quarterly results.

Outlook

  • The high-yield market performed well in 2025. As we head into 2026, corporate fundamentals have been resilient and technicals in the market have been supportive. We expect these trends to persist, with bouts of volatility surrounding the implementation of President Trump’s policies.
  • From a valuation standpoint, spreads remain rich historically, but are appropriate given the low default environment supported by wide open capital markets. The Fed implemented 75 bps of cuts in the second half of the year as the labor market softened and growth slowed but remained positive.
  • We remain focused on industry and company fundamentals, the health of balance sheets, generation and use of free cash flow, the resiliency of credits to slower economic growth, and are cautious on deeply distressed names. The U.S. high-yield default rate (including distressed exchanges) increased in December, but remains well below long-term averages, ending the month at 1.88%.
  • Technicals remain solid with strong demand, inflows and consistent new issues. Yields and sub-par dollar prices remain somewhat attractive, while spreads remain at levels that are fair but not compelling.
  • Risks to our outlook include higher than expected inflation, policy missteps, a more severe slowdown or recession, and/or heightened geopolitical tension.

As of 12/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 5 star and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 584, 548 and 445 High Yield Bond funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.
